# Classical m3=1 proof note

This note starts the standalone extraction of the classical m3=1 branch from
`NONABELIAN.md`.  It closes the A1 central-quotient leaf and records the
remaining classical-family obligations by name.

## Proposition 1 (central quotient transfer)

Let `p : G -> H` be a continuous surjective homomorphism of compact connected
Lie groups with finite central kernel.  Let `chi_H` be the adjoint character
of `H`, and let `chi_G = chi_H o p`.  For every integer `n >= 0`,

```text
Q3^G(n) = Q3^H(n),
```

where both Q3 integrals use normalized Haar probability measure and the
corresponding adjoint character.

Proof.  The pushforward of normalized Haar measure on `G` under `p` is the
normalized Haar measure on `H`, since it is a left-invariant probability
measure on `H`.  The central kernel acts trivially in the adjoint
representation, so the adjoint character of `G` factors as `chi_H o p`.
Therefore the integrand

```text
(chi_G(g) - chi_G(h))^2 (chi_G(g) + chi_G(h))^n
```

is the pullback along `p x p` of the corresponding integrand on `H x H`.
Changing variables by the Haar pushforward gives the displayed equality.

## Proposition 2 (A1 adjoint Q3 branch)

For every compact connected simple group with Lie algebra of type `A1` and
for every integer `n >= 0`,

```text
Q3^A1(n) >= 0.
```

Proof.  The simply connected compact group of type `A1` is `SU(2)`, and every
compact connected group with Lie algebra `A1` is a central quotient of
`SU(2)`.  By Proposition 1, the adjoint Q3 value is unchanged under that
central quotient.  Applying `SU_N_REPAIR.md`, Theorem 71, at `N = 2` gives
`Q3^{SU(2),adj}(n) >= 0` for every `n >= 0`, and hence the same inequality
for every compact connected group of type `A1`.

## Proposition 3 (classical symmetric-cubic degree table)

Among the classical compact simple adjoint families, the symmetric invariant
cubic on the Lie algebra occurs exactly for `A_n` with `n >= 2`.  It does not
occur for `A1`, `B_n`, `C_n`, or `D_n` with `D_3` identified with `A_3`.

```text
A_n, n >= 2: degree 3 present.
A1: degree 3 absent.
B_n: degree 3 absent.
C_n: degree 3 absent.
D_n, n >= 4: degree 3 absent.
```

Proof.  Zuber 2012, §3.2.4, equation (3.39), states that adding `1` to the
Coxeter exponents gives the degrees of the algebraically independent Casimir
operators.  Zuber 2012, Appendix F, lists the classical exponents:

```text
A_{N-1}: 1,2,...,N-1.
B_l:     1,3,5,...,2l-1.
C_l:     1,3,5,...,2l-1.
D_l:     1,3,5,...,2l-3,l-1.
```

Therefore the corresponding invariant degrees are:

```text
A_{N-1}: 2,3,...,N.
B_l:     2,4,6,...,2l.
C_l:     2,4,6,...,2l.
D_l:     2,4,6,...,2l-2,l.
```

The degree `3` appears only in `A_{N-1}` with `N >= 3`, equivalently `A_n`
with `n >= 2`.  It is absent for `A1`, for all `B_l`, for all `C_l`, and for
`D_l` with `l >= 4`; the low-rank coincidence `D_3 = A_3` belongs to the
already routed `A_n` branch.

## Corollary 4 (classical routing after SU and A1)

For the classical compact simple adjoint families, the remaining non-SU,
non-`A1` branch is exactly

```text
B_n = SO(2n+1),  C_n = Sp(n),  D_n = SO(2n), n >= 4.
```

Proof.  The `A_n` branch with `n >= 2` is `SU(N)` with `N >= 3`, and is
handled by `SU_N_REPAIR.md`, Theorem 71.  The `A1` branch is handled by
Proposition 2.  Proposition 3 removes every other classical family from the
symmetric-cubic branch, leaving the displayed `B`, `C`, and `D` families for
the m3=1 classical tail slot.

## Proposition 5 (classical adjoint trace formulas)

Let `T_j(U) = Tr(U^j)` in the defining representation.

For `SO(N)`, `N >= 3`, the adjoint character is

```text
chi_adj^{SO}(U) = (T_1(U)^2 - T_2(U))/2.
```

For `Sp(n) = USp(2n)`, `n >= 1`, the adjoint character is

```text
chi_adj^{Sp}(U) = (T_1(U)^2 + T_2(U))/2.
```

Proof.  The character of the exterior square of a representation with
character `T_1` is `(T_1^2 - T_2)/2`, and the character of the symmetric
square is `(T_1^2 + T_2)/2`.  The complexified adjoint representation of
`SO(N)` is `Lambda^2` of the defining representation, while the complexified
adjoint representation of `USp(2n)` is `Sym^2` of the defining
representation.  Substitution gives the two displayed formulas.

## Proposition 6 (stable SO/Sp adjoint moment equality)

Define the stable orthogonal and symplectic adjoint random variables by the
Diaconis-Shahshahani trace limits

```text
X_SO = (Z_1^2 - 1 - sqrt(2) Z_2)/2,
X_Sp = (Z_1^2 - 1 + sqrt(2) Z_2)/2,
```

where `Z_1,Z_2` are independent standard normal variables.  Then

```text
E[X_SO^k] = E[X_Sp^k]
```

for every integer `k >= 0`.

Proof.  Diaconis-Shahshahani 1994, Theorem 4, gives the orthogonal stable
trace limits `T_1 -> Z_1` and `T_2 -> 1 + sqrt(2) Z_2`; Theorem 6 gives the
symplectic stable trace limits `T_1 -> Z_1` and
`T_2 -> -1 + sqrt(2) Z_2`.  Proposition 5 gives exactly the displayed
variables.  Since `Z_2` and `-Z_2` have the same distribution, `X_SO` and
`X_Sp` have the same distribution.  Therefore all moments agree.

Equivalently, for `|t| < 1`,

```text
E[exp(t X_SO)] = E[exp(t X_Sp)]
               = exp(t^2/4 - t/2) / sqrt(1 - t).
```

Indeed,

```text
E[exp(t(Z_1^2 - 1 +/- sqrt(2) Z_2)/2)]
  = exp(-t/2) E[exp(t Z_1^2/2)] E[exp(+- t sqrt(2) Z_2/2)]
  = exp(-t/2) (1-t)^(-1/2) exp(t^2/4).
```

## Corollary 7 (Sp stable branch reduces to SO stable branch)

Any stable-chain inequality stated purely in terms of the stable adjoint
moments holds for `Sp(infinity)` if and only if it holds for `SO(infinity)`.

Proof.  The exact finite formula for `Q3(n)` is polynomial in the moments
`m_0,...,m_{n+2}`.  Proposition 6 identifies the full stable moment sequence
for `SO(infinity)` and `Sp(infinity)`, so every stable `Q3(n)` and every
stable Chain difference agrees in the two stable limits.

## Proposition 8 (finite Q3 moment formula)

Let `X` and `Y` be independent copies of a real random variable with moments
`m_j = E[X^j]`.  Define

```text
Q3(n) = E[(X - Y)^2 (X + Y)^n].
```

Then, for every integer `n >= 0`,

```text
Q3(n)
  = 2 sum_{k=0}^n binom(n,k)
      (m_{k+2} m_{n-k} - m_{k+1} m_{n-k+1}).
```

In particular, `Q3(n)` depends only on `m_0,...,m_{n+2}`.

Proof.  Expand `(X+Y)^n` by the binomial theorem:

```text
Q3(n)
 = sum_{k=0}^n binom(n,k)
   E[(X^2 - 2XY + Y^2) X^k Y^{n-k}]
 = sum_{k=0}^n binom(n,k)
   (m_{k+2}m_{n-k} - 2m_{k+1}m_{n-k+1} + m_k m_{n-k+2}).
```

In the final term, replace `k` by `n-k` and use
`binom(n,k)=binom(n,n-k)`.  This identifies its sum with the first-term sum,
giving the displayed formula.  The largest moment index appearing is `n+2`.

## Corollary 9 (Chain step moment range)

Let

```text
D(r) = Q3(2r+3) - 4 Q3(2r+1).
```

Then `D(r)` depends only on the moments

```text
m_0,...,m_{2r+5}.
```

Consequently, if two adjoint-character moment sequences agree through
`m_{2r+5}`, then their Chain step `D(r)` is identical.

Proof.  Proposition 8 says that `Q3(2r+1)` uses moments through `m_{2r+3}`
and `Q3(2r+3)` uses moments through `m_{2r+5}`.  The displayed Chain
difference therefore uses no moment beyond `m_{2r+5}`.  Equality of the two
moment sequences through that index gives equality of both Q3 terms and hence
of `D(r)`.

## Proposition 10 (stable SO Chain generating function)

For the stable `SO(infinity)` adjoint variable of Proposition 6, let

```text
Z(t) = E[exp(t X_SO)] = exp(t^2/4 - t/2) / sqrt(1 - t),
F(t) = sum_{n>=0} Q3_stab(n) t^n/n!.
```

Then

```text
F(t) = ((1-t)^(-3) + (1-t)^(-1)) exp(-t + t^2/2).
```

Moreover, if

```text
G(t) = exp(-t + t^2/2),
P(t) = 12(1-t)^(-5) - 7(1-t)^(-3) - 4(1-t)^(-1) + (1-t),
```

then

```text
F''(t) - 4F(t) = P(t) G(t).
```

Proof.  For independent copies `X,Y` with moment-generating function `Z`,

```text
F(t) = E[(X-Y)^2 exp(t(X+Y))]
     = 2(Z''(t)Z(t) - Z'(t)^2)
     = 2Z(t)^2 (log Z(t))''.
```

Here

```text
log Z(t) = t^2/4 - t/2 - (1/2) log(1-t),
(log Z(t))'' = 1/2 + 1/(2(1-t)^2).
```

Thus

```text
F(t) = Z(t)^2 (1 + (1-t)^(-2))
     = ((1-t)^(-1) + (1-t)^(-3)) exp(-t + t^2/2).
```

Set `u=1-t` and write `F(t)=A(t)G(t)` with

```text
A(t) = u^(-1) + u^(-3),
G'(t)/G(t) = t-1 = -u,
G''(t)/G(t) = (t-1)^2 + 1 = u^2 + 1.
```

Then

```text
(F''(t)-4F(t))/G(t)
  = A''(t) + 2(t-1)A'(t) + ((t-1)^2-3)A(t).
```

Since

```text
A'(t) = u^(-2) + 3u^(-4),
A''(t) = 2u^(-3) + 12u^(-5),
```

the right side is

```text
2u^(-3) + 12u^(-5)
  - 2u(u^(-2) + 3u^(-4))
  + (u^2-3)(u^(-1) + u^(-3))
= 12u^(-5) - 7u^(-3) - 4u^(-1) + u.
```

Substituting `u=1-t` gives the stated identity for `F'' - 4F`.

## Lemma 11 (stable coefficient monotonicities)

Write

```text
P(t) = sum_{k>=0} p_k t^k,
G(t) = sum_{k>=0} g_k t^k,
g_k = (-1)^k h_k.
```

Then `p_{k+1} > p_k` for every `k >= 0`, and
`h_k >= h_{k+1} > 0` for every `k >= 0`.

Proof.  The coefficients of `P` are

```text
p_k = 12 binom(k+4,4) - 7 binom(k+2,2) - 4 + [k=0] - [k=1].
```

Hence `p_1-p_0=32`, `p_2-p_1=100`, and for `k >= 2`,

```text
p_{k+1}-p_k
  = 12 binom(k+4,3) - 7(k+2)
  = (k+2)(2(k+3)(k+4)-7) > 0.
```

For `h_k`, use

```text
exp(t + t^2/2) = sum_{k>=0} h_k t^k.
```

The coefficients are positive and satisfy

```text
(k+1)h_{k+1} = h_k + h_{k-1},   h_{-1}=0, h_0=1.
```

For `k >= 1`,

```text
h_k - h_{k+1}
  = (k h_k - h_{k-1})/(k+1)
  = h_{k-2}/(k+1) >= 0,
```

where the last equality uses the same recurrence at index `k-1`.  Also
`h_0=h_1=1`, so `h_k >= h_{k+1}` for every `k >= 0`.

## Proposition 12 (stable SO Chain)

For every integer `r >= 0`,

```text
Q3_stab(2r+3) - 4 Q3_stab(2r+1) > 0.
```

Proof.  Let `N=2r+1`.  By Proposition 10,

```text
Q3_stab(N+2) - 4 Q3_stab(N)
  = N! [t^N] P(t)G(t)
  = N! sum_{k=0}^N p_k g_{N-k}.
```

Since `N` is odd and `g_j=(-1)^j h_j`, pair even and odd `k`:

```text
sum_{k=0}^N p_k g_{N-k}
  = sum_{j=0}^{(N-1)/2}
      (p_{2j+1} h_{N-2j-1} - p_{2j} h_{N-2j}).
```

For each `j`, Lemma 11 gives

```text
p_{2j+1} > p_{2j},
h_{N-2j-1} >= h_{N-2j} > 0.
```

Therefore every paired summand is positive:

```text
p_{2j+1} h_{N-2j-1} - p_{2j} h_{N-2j} > 0.
```

Multiplication by `N! > 0` preserves positivity, proving the displayed Chain
inequality.

## Corollary 13 (stable Sp Chain)

For every integer `r >= 0`,

```text
Q3_stab,Sp(2r+3) - 4 Q3_stab,Sp(2r+1) > 0.
```

Proof.  Corollary 7 identifies every stable symplectic Chain difference with
the corresponding stable orthogonal Chain difference.  Proposition 12 gives
the orthogonal positivity.

## Lemma 14 (low-degree SO and O trace averages agree)

Let `P(U)` be a polynomial in the matrix entries of the defining
representation of `SO(N)`, of total matrix-entry degree `d < N`.  Then

```text
int_{SO(N)} P(U) dU = int_{O(N)} P(U) dU.
```

Proof.  Write the degree-`d` part of `P` as a contraction against
`U^{tensor d}`; lower-degree parts are identical with smaller `d`.
The tensor

```text
M_SO(d) = int_{SO(N)} U^{tensor d} dU
```

is invariant under left and right multiplication by `SO(N)`.  It is enough to
show that it is also invariant under one orientation-reversing reflection.

Fix the standard basis.  For any two distinct coordinate axes `a,b`, the
diagonal matrix changing the signs of those two axes lies in `SO(N)`.
Applying this sign change on the left of `M_SO(d)` shows that any nonzero
component has the property that the sum of the row-index multiplicities of
`a` and `b` is even for every pair `a,b`.  Therefore all row-index
multiplicities have the same parity.  If all of them were odd, their sum
would be at least `N`; this is impossible because there are only `d < N` row
indices.  Thus all row multiplicities are even, so changing the sign of one
coordinate on the left fixes every component.  The same argument applied on
the right handles the column indices.

So `M_SO(d)` is fixed by a coordinate reflection on the left and on the
right.  Since `O(N)` is generated by `SO(N)` and such a reflection,
averaging over the other component of `O(N)` gives the same tensor.  The
displayed integral identity follows after contracting with the coefficient
tensor of `P`.

## Proposition 15 (stable-rank adjoint moment stabilization)

Let `m_s^G = E_G[chi_adj^s]`.

For `B_b = SO(2b+1)`, if `b >= floor(s/2)`, then

```text
m_s^{B_b} = m_s^{SO(infinity)}.
```

For `D_b = SO(2b)`, if `b >= s+1`, then

```text
m_s^{D_b} = m_s^{SO(infinity)}.
```

For `C_b = Sp(b)`, if `b >= floor(s/2)`, then

```text
m_s^{C_b} = m_s^{Sp(infinity)}.
```

Proof.  By Proposition 5,

```text
chi_adj^{SO}(U)^s
  = 2^{-s} sum_{j=0}^s binom(s,j)(-1)^{s-j} T_1(U)^{2j} T_2(U)^{s-j},
chi_adj^{Sp}(U)^s
  = 2^{-s} sum_{j=0}^s binom(s,j) T_1(U)^{2j} T_2(U)^{s-j}.
```

First handle `B_b`.  The adjoint character of `SO(2b+1)` is the Schur
function `s_(1,1)` in the defining eigenvalues.  Since `s_(1,1)` is homogeneous
of degree two, `s_(1,1)(-U)^s=s_(1,1)(U)^s`.  For odd matrix size
`N=2b+1`, the other component of `O(N)` is `(-I) SO(N)`, so

```text
int_{SO(N)} s_(1,1)^s = int_{O(N)} s_(1,1)^s.
```

Expand

```text
s_(1,1)^s = sum_lambda c_lambda s_lambda.
```

Rains 1998, Section 3, states the orthogonal Schur integral criterion:

```text
int_{O(N)} s_lambda = 1
```

exactly when `lambda` has at most `N` parts and every row length of `lambda` is
even; otherwise the integral is zero.  In the product above every partition has
size `2s`.  If it can contribute to the orthogonal integral, every nonzero row
has length at least two, so

```text
ell(lambda) <= s.
```

Thus `b >= floor(s/2)` gives `N=2b+1 >= s`, so every contributing Schur
summand is present and has the same integral as in the stable orthogonal
limit.

For `D_b`, Diaconis-Shahshahani 1994, Theorem 4, p.56, gives the exact
orthogonal trace-moment identity for every monomial

```text
T_1^{a_1} ... T_k^{a_k}
```

when the matrix size is at least the weighted trace degree
`a_1+2a_2+...+k a_k`, and identifies it with the stable independent-normal
trace model used in Proposition 6.  Each summand in the orthogonal expansion
above has

```text
a_1 + 2a_2 = 2j + 2(s-j) = 2s.
```

For `D_b`, the defining matrix size is `N=2b`; `b >= s+1` gives `2s < N`.
Thus the Diaconis-Shahshahani orthogonal identity applies, and Lemma 14
transfers the `O(N)` trace averages to `SO(N)` because each summand has
matrix-entry degree at most `2s < N`.  Substituting `T_1 -> Z_1` and
`T_2 -> 1 + sqrt(2) Z_2` gives the stable `SO(infinity)` variable of
Proposition 6.

For `C_b`, use the symplectic Schur-function form.  Proposition 5 identifies
the adjoint representation of `Sp(b)` with `Sym^2(V)`, whose character is the
Schur function `s_(2)` in the defining eigenvalues.  Hence

```text
m_s^{C_b} = int_{Sp(b)} s_(2)^s.
```

By Pieri's rule, every Schur function `s_lambda` occurring in the product
`s_(2)^s` has `|lambda|=2s` and at most `s` nonzero parts, since each
multiplication by `s_(2)` adds a horizontal two-strip and can create at most one
new row.

Rains 1998, Section 3, states the symplectic Schur integral criterion:

```text
int_{Sp(2b)} s_lambda = 1
```

exactly when `lambda` has at most `2b` parts and every row length occurs with
even multiplicity; otherwise the integral is zero.  If a partition `lambda`
occurring in `s_(2)^s` satisfies this even-multiplicity condition, then the
number of nonzero parts `ell(lambda)` is even.  Since `ell(lambda) <= s`, this
gives

```text
ell(lambda) <= 2 floor(s/2).
```

Thus `b >= floor(s/2)` implies `ell(lambda) <= 2b` for every Schur summand that
can contribute to the symplectic integral.  The finite-rank integral therefore
agrees term-by-term with the stable symplectic integral.

## Corollary 16 (stable-rank classical Chain)

For every integer `r >= 0`, the Chain difference

```text
Q3^G(2r+3) - 4 Q3^G(2r+1)
```

is positive in the following stable-rank regions:

```text
B_b = SO(2b+1):  b >= r+2,
D_b = SO(2b):    b >= 2r+6,
C_b = Sp(b):     b >= r+2.
```

Proof.  Corollary 9 shows that Chain step `r` uses only
`m_0,...,m_{2r+5}`.  Proposition 15 identifies all of these moments with the
corresponding stable moment sequence in the displayed rank ranges.  The
orthogonal cases therefore equal the stable `SO(infinity)` Chain difference,
which is positive by Proposition 12.  The symplectic case equals the stable
`Sp(infinity)` Chain difference, which is positive by Corollary 13.

## Lemma 16A.1 (SO tensor invariants at determinant degree)

Let `V=R^N` with `N >= 3`.  Let `epsilon` be the oriented volume tensor in
`V^(tensor N)`.  Then

```text
(V^(tensor N))^SO(N) = (V^(tensor N))^O(N) direct_sum span(epsilon).
```

If `N=2b`, the blockwise antisymmetric projection from `V^(tensor 2b)` to
`(Lambda^2 V)^(tensor b)` sends `epsilon` to a nonzero tensor.

Proof.  Let `R` be the coordinate reflection changing the sign of `e_1`.
Since `O(N)` is generated by `SO(N)` and `R`, the `+1` eigenspace of `R` on
`(V^(tensor N))^SO(N)` is exactly `(V^(tensor N))^O(N)`.  It remains to show
that the `-1` eigenspace is one-dimensional.

Let `T` be an `SO(N)`-invariant tensor with `R T=-T`.  Write components in the
standard basis as `T_{i_1,...,i_N}`.  For any diagonal sign change
`diag(delta_1,...,delta_N)` with determinant `1`, invariance gives

```text
T_{i_1,...,i_N}
  = delta_{i_1} ... delta_{i_N} T_{i_1,...,i_N}.
```

Thus a nonzero component has coordinate multiplicities whose parities are all
equal.  The condition `R T=-T` forces the first coordinate multiplicity to be
odd in every nonzero component, so all coordinate multiplicities are odd.  Since
their sum is `N`, every coordinate appears exactly once.

For `i<j`, use the rotation in the `(i,j)`-plane given by

```text
e_i |-> e_j,   e_j |-> -e_i,
```

and fixing the other coordinate axes.  This rotation lies in `SO(N)`.  Applying
it to a component in which all labels occur once gives

```text
T_{...,i,...,j,...} = - T_{...,j,...,i,...}.
```

Transpositions generate the symmetric group, hence the nonzero components are
alternating in the coordinate labels.  The `-1` eigenspace is therefore spanned
by `epsilon`.

For `N=2b`, the blockwise antisymmetric projection of `epsilon` is the Pfaffian
multilinear form

```text
(omega_1,...,omega_b) |-> coefficient of e_1 wedge ... wedge e_{2b}
                       in omega_1 wedge ... wedge omega_b.
```

It takes value `1` on
`(e_1 wedge e_2, e_3 wedge e_4, ..., e_{2b-1} wedge e_{2b})`, so it is nonzero.

## Lemma 16A (first D-boundary adjoint moment)

For `D_b=SO(2b)`, let `m_s^{D_b}` be the adjoint-character moment and let
`m_s^stab` be the stable `SO(infinity)` adjoint moment.  Then

```text
m_s^{D_b} = m_s^stab       for 0 <= s <= b-1,
m_b^{D_b} = m_b^stab + 1.
```

Proof.  The first line is the `D_b` case of Proposition 15.

For the boundary moment, write `V=R^(2b)` and complexify the representations.
The adjoint representation of `SO(2b)` is `Lambda^2 V`.  The Haar average on
`(Lambda^2 V)^(tensor b)` is the projection onto the invariant subspace, and
the trace of that projection is the integral of the tensor-power character.
Therefore

```text
m_b^{D_b} = dim ((Lambda^2 V)^(tensor b))^SO(2b).
```

For `O(2b)`, the same character is `(T_1^2-T_2)/2`.  Expanding its `b`th power
as in Proposition 15, every summand has weighted trace degree `2b`.  Therefore
Diaconis-Shahshahani 1994, Theorem 4, p.56, applies at equality and gives

```text
dim ((Lambda^2 V)^(tensor b))^O(2b) = m_b^stab.
```

Embedding `(Lambda^2 V)^(tensor b)` into `V^(tensor 2b)`, Lemma 16A.1 says
that the possible `SO(2b)` invariants not already fixed by `O(2b)` form at most
one line, represented by the volume tensor.  The blockwise antisymmetrizer is
`O(2b)`-equivariant, and the final assertion of Lemma 16A.1 shows that the
volume line has nonzero image in `(Lambda^2 V)^(tensor b)`.
Thus the `SO(2b)` invariant space has exactly one additional dimension beyond
the `O(2b)` stable contribution, proving
`m_b^{D_b}=m_b^stab+1`.

## Lemma 16B (D-boundary adjoint moments through depth four)

For `D_b=SO(2b)`, `b>=4`, put

```text
Delta_j(b) = m_{b+j}^{D_b} - m_{b+j}^stab.
```

Then

```text
Delta_0(b) = 1,
Delta_1(b) = binom(b,2),
Delta_2(b) = b(b+1)(b^2+b+2)/8,
Delta_3(b) =
  (b-1)(b+2)(b^4+8b^3+25b^2+18b-24)/48,
Delta_4(b) =
  (b+3)(b^7+17b^6+103b^5+203b^4
         -104b^3-460b^2-48b+384)/384.
```

Proof.  Let `V=R^(2b)`, `W=Lambda^2 V`, and `N=2b`.  For
`s=b,b+1,b+2,b+3,b+4`, the `O(N)` invariant contribution already equals the
stable contribution.  Indeed, by the Rains 1998 Section 3 orthogonal Schur
criterion, an `O(N)`-contributing Schur summand has even row lengths.  Since
every summand in `e_2^s` has total size `2s`, such a partition has at most `s`
nonzero rows; and `s <= 2b=N` because `b>=4`.

Thus `Delta_j(b)` is the difference between the `SO(N)` and `O(N)` invariant
multiplicities in `W^(tensor s)`.  This difference is the determinant-isotypic
multiplicity for `O(N)`:

```text
dim (W^(tensor s))^SO(N) - dim (W^(tensor s))^O(N)
  = int_{O(N)} det(U) chi_W(U)^s dU.
```

In symmetric-function notation `chi_W=e_2`.  Expand

```text
e_2^s = sum_lambda c_s(lambda) s_lambda.
```

Rains 1998, Section 3, gives the orthogonal Schur integral criterion; after
tensoring by `det`, the contributing Schur summands are exactly those `lambda`
with precisely `N` odd parts.  Equivalently, for `s=b+j`, the contributing
partitions are

```text
lambda = (1^N) + 2 nu,        nu partition j.
```

The Pieri rule gives the recurrence

```text
c_{r+1}(lambda) =
  sum_{mu : lambda/mu is a vertical two-strip} c_r(mu),
  c_0(empty)=1.
```

For `j=0`, the only partition is `(1^N)`, and the recurrence gives coefficient
`1`.

For `j=1`, the only partition is `(3,1^(N-1))`; substituting it into the same
vertical-strip recurrence gives

```text
c_{b+1}(3,1^(2b-1)) = binom(b,2).
```

For `j=2`, the contributing partitions are

```text
(5,1^(2b-1)),        (3,3,1^(2b-2)).
```

The recurrence gives

```text
c_{b+2}(5,1^(2b-1))       = binom(b+1,4),
c_{b+2}(3,3,1^(2b-2))     = b(b+1)^2(b+2)/12.
```

Adding these two coefficients gives

```text
binom(b+1,4) + b(b+1)^2(b+2)/12
  = b(b+1)(b^2+b+2)/8.
```

For `j=3`, the contributing partitions are

```text
(7,1^(2b-1)),        (5,3,1^(2b-2)),        (3,3,3,1^(2b-3)).
```

The recurrence gives

```text
c_{b+3}(7,1^(2b-1))       = binom(b+2,6),
c_{b+3}(5,3,1^(2b-2))     = b(b-1)(b+1)(b+2)^2(b+3)/80,
c_{b+3}(3,3,3,1^(2b-3))   =
  (b-1)(b+2)(b+3)(b+4)(b^2+7b-6)/144.
```

Their sum simplifies to the displayed formula for `Delta_3(b)`.

For `j=4`, the contributing partitions are

```text
(9,1^(2b-1)),        (7,3,1^(2b-2)),
(5,5,1^(2b-2)),      (5,3,3,1^(2b-3)),
(3,3,3,3,1^(2b-4)).
```

The recurrence gives

```text
c_{b+4}(9,1^(2b-1)) =
  b(b-4)(b-3)(b-2)(b-1)(b+1)(b+2)(b+3)/40320,

c_{b+4}(7,3,1^(2b-2)) =
  b(b-2)(b-1)(b+1)(b+2)(b+3)^2(b+4)/2016,

c_{b+4}(5,5,1^(2b-2)) =
  b(b+1)^2(b+2)^2(b+3)^2(b+4)/2880,

c_{b+4}(5,3,3,1^(2b-3)) =
  b(b-1)(b+1)(b+3)(b+4)(b^3+15b^2+50b-12)/720,

c_{b+4}(3,3,3,3,1^(2b-4)) =
  (b-1)(b+2)(b+3)(b+4)(b^4+24b^3+137b^2-6b-360)/2880.
```

Their sum simplifies to the displayed formula for `Delta_4(b)`.

## Lemma 16C (finite D-boundary Pieri recurrence)

For `D_b=SO(2b)`, `b>=4`, and `0<=j<=b`, put

```text
Delta_j(b) = m_{b+j}^{D_b} - m_{b+j}^stab.
```

Let

```text
e_2^r = sum_lambda c_r(lambda) s_lambda
```

and define `c_0(empty)=1`, with the Pieri recurrence

```text
c_{r+1}(lambda) =
  sum_{mu : lambda/mu is a vertical two-strip} c_r(mu).
```

Then

```text
Delta_j(b) =
  sum_{nu partition j} c_{b+j}((1^(2b)) + 2nu).
```

Proof.  The same determinant-isotypic argument as in Lemma 16B gives

```text
Delta_j(b) = int_{O(2b)} det(U) e_2(U)^(b+j) dU,
```

because the `O(2b)` invariant contribution equals the stable contribution when
`j<=b`: every `O(2b)`-contributing even-row Schur summand of `e_2^(b+j)` has at
most `b+j<=2b` nonzero rows.  Rains 1998, Section 3, applied after tensoring by
`det`, says that the Schur summands contributing to the displayed determinant
integral are exactly those with `2b` odd parts.  Since their size is
`2(b+j)`, they are precisely

```text
(1^(2b)) + 2nu,        nu partition j.
```

The coefficients of `e_2^r` are obtained by multiplying by the elementary
symmetric function `e_2`; Pieri's rule is exactly the displayed vertical-strip
recurrence.  Summing the contributing coefficients gives the formula.

## Lemma 16C.1 (finite D decomposition beyond the stable O-window)

For `D_b=SO(2b)`, `b>=4`, and every `s>=0`, write

```text
e_2^s = sum_lambda c_s(lambda) s_lambda,
```

where the coefficients `c_s(lambda)` are defined by the vertical two-strip
Pieri recurrence of Lemma 16C.  Put

```text
A_s(b) =
  sum_{nu partition s, ell(nu)>2b}
      c_s(2nu_1,2nu_2,...),
```

and

```text
B_s(b) = 0                                                    if s < b,
B_s(b) =
  sum_{nu partition s-b, ell(nu)<=2b}
      c_s(1+2nu_1,...,1+2nu_ell(nu),1^(2b-ell(nu)))           if s >= b.
```

Then

```text
m_s^{D_b} = m_s^stab - A_s(b) + B_s(b).
```

Proof.  Let `N=2b`, `V=R^N`, and `W=Lambda^2 V`.  Since `SO(N)` is a normal
subgroup of `O(N)` with quotient `{+-1}`, the `SO(N)`-invariant subspace of
`W^(tensor s)` decomposes into the `O(N)`-fixed part and the determinant
isotypic part.  Equivalently,

```text
m_s^{D_b}
  = int_{O(N)} e_2(U)^s dU + int_{O(N)} det(U) e_2(U)^s dU.
```

Expand `e_2^s` in Schur functions as above.  Rains 1998, Section 3, gives
the orthogonal Schur integral criterion: the first integral keeps exactly the
Schur summands whose row lengths are even and whose length is at most `N`.
The stable orthogonal moment keeps all even-row summands.  Hence the first
integral equals `m_s^stab - A_s(b)`.

For the second integral, tensoring by `det` changes the Rains criterion to
the determinant-isotypic criterion: the contributing Schur summands are
exactly those with length at most `N` and with all `N` rows odd after padding.
Such a partition has size at least `N`; if `s<b` there is no contribution.
If `s>=b`, writing each odd row as `1+2nu_i` gives exactly the partitions
displayed in `B_s(b)`, with `nu` a partition of `s-b` and `ell(nu)<=N`.
The Schur coefficients are the Pieri coefficients `c_s(lambda)` from Lemma
16C.  Combining the two integrals gives the displayed identity.

### Type-D correction audit notice (2026-07-13)

The exact correction relative to the stable moment is `B_s(b)-A_s(b)`.
Historical replay text later in this file often calls the nonnegative
determinant-isotypic quantity `B_s(b)` a “determinant correction” and prints it
as `Delta_s`.  That artifact name does **not** make it the full finite-rank
correction once `s>2b`, when `A_s(b)` can be nonzero.

Consequently, every historical lower-bound argument that discards
positive-coefficient terms using `Delta_s>=0`, or substitutes `B_s(b)` or the
stable moment as an upper envelope for the full correction, used the proposed
finite statement `DEnv`:

```text
0 <= B_s(b)-A_s(b) <= m_s^stable
```

over the moment indices used by that certificate.  `DEnv` is now known to be
false: the exact C++/GMP value at `D_4,j=18` is
`B_18-A_18=-80781084484224`.  Exact Weyl and exact row-capped moment replays
are unaffected.  The main paper replaces the false-envelope route
unconditionally.  For `D_4..D_24` it uses machine-C exact moments, all 530
row-gated GMP Chain checks, an exact-rational `D_10` tail, an exact-MGF onset
and exact bridge for `D_11`, and directed-MPFR exact-moment `D_12..D_24`
tails.  Exact/mixed finite-rank MGFs,
two-sided correction boxes, the Rains determinant-component identity, a
directed layered-MGF onset certificate, and an exact A-free GMP frontier
cover `D_25..D_295`.  Later historical claims in this file do not override
that current status.

### Low-rank B/C normalization audit notice (2026-07-13)

Historical low-rank B/C tail sections later in this file are not the active
terminal proof.  The type-B Macdonald--Mehta cap coefficient omitted the
actual-short-root factor `2^{-b}`, and the type-C cap normalization did not
justify the claimed rows.  The main paper retains corrected caps only for
`B_4,B_5,B_6,B_8` and replaces `B_7,B_9..B_13,C_4..C_19` by exact
Toeplitz--Hankel determinant MGFs plus a short exact-integer onset bridge.
The old tail logs are classified as superseded.

## Lemma 16C.2 (moment-interval Chain lower bound)

Fix an integer `r>=0`.  Let `m_0,...,m_{2r+5}` be a real moment vector and
suppose that interval bounds

```text
L_s <= m_s <= U_s,        0 <= s <= 2r+5,
```

are known.  For an integer coefficient `c` and indices `a,b`, put

```text
I_c(a,b) =
  c min{L_a L_b, L_a U_b, U_a L_b, U_a U_b}       if c >= 0,
  c max{L_a L_b, L_a U_b, U_a L_b, U_a U_b}       if c < 0.
```

Then `Q3(2r+3)-4Q3(2r+1)` is bounded below by

```text
sum_{n in {2r+3,2r+1}} alpha_n
  sum_{k=0}^n 2 binom(n,k)
    (m_{k+2} m_{n-k} - m_{k+1} m_{n-k+1})
```

after replacing each product term `c m_a m_b` by `I_c(a,b)`, where
`alpha_{2r+3}=1`, `alpha_{2r+1}=-4`, and `c` is the full coefficient of that
product.  Equivalently, the lower bound is the interval-arithmetic lower
endpoint obtained from Proposition 8.  If this lower endpoint is positive,
then `Q3(2r+3)-4Q3(2r+1)>0`.

Proof.  For any `x in [L_a,U_a]` and `y in [L_b,U_b]`, the bilinear product
`xy` lies between the minimum and maximum of its four endpoint products.
Multiplying by a nonnegative coefficient preserves the lower endpoint, while
multiplying by a negative coefficient reverses it.  Applying this to every
product `m_a m_b` in the exact formula of Proposition 8 and summing the
resulting lower bounds gives the displayed lower bound.  Positivity of that
lower bound implies positivity of the Chain difference.

## Lemma 16C.3 (D determinant-suffix moment enclosure)

Let `D_b=SO(2b)` with `b>=4`, and keep the notation `A_s(b),B_s(b)` of
Lemma 16C.1.  Suppose that `B_s(b)` has been computed exactly for a moment
index `s`.  Then

```text
B_s(b) <= m_s^{D_b} <= m_s^stab + B_s(b).
```

Consequently, if exact moment values are known on a prefix and the
determinant-suffix values `B_s(b)` are known on the remaining moments needed
by Corollary 9, then Lemma 16C.2 gives a certified lower bound for the
corresponding Chain difference from those mixed exact/interval data.

Proof.  Lemma 16C.1 gives

```text
m_s^{D_b} = m_s^stab - A_s(b) + B_s(b).
```

The Pieri coefficients `c_s(lambda)` are nonnegative because they count
vertical two-strip Pieri paths.  Thus `B_s(b)>=0`.  Also `A_s(b)` is the sum
of a subset of the even-row Pieri coefficients whose full sum is
`m_s^stab`; hence

```text
0 <= A_s(b) <= m_s^stab.
```

Substitution gives the displayed lower and upper bounds.  Applying Lemma
16C.2 to the exact prefix intervals and these suffix intervals gives the
final assertion.

## Lemma 16C.4 (row-capped D suffix exact replay)

Let `D_b=SO(2b)` with `b>=4`, put `N=2b`, and let `c_s(lambda)` be the
Pieri coefficients of Lemma 16C.  For each `s`, define

```text
K_s(b) =
  sum_{nu partition s, ell(nu)<=N}
      c_s(2nu_1,2nu_2,...)
```

and keep `B_s(b)` from Lemma 16C.1.  Then

```text
m_s^{D_b} = K_s(b) + B_s(b).
```

Moreover, the coefficients entering `K_s(b)` and `B_s(b)` are obtained
exactly by the forward Pieri recurrence after discarding every intermediate
partition with more than `N` rows.

Proof.  Lemma 16C.1 gives

```text
m_s^{D_b}=m_s^stab-A_s(b)+B_s(b).
```

The stable moment is the sum of `c_s(2nu)` over all partitions `nu` of `s`.
The term `A_s(b)` is exactly the sub-sum with `ell(nu)>N`.  Therefore
`m_s^stab-A_s(b)=K_s(b)`, proving the displayed identity.

It remains to justify the row-capped replay.  In one Pieri step for
multiplication by `e_2`, the partition changes by adding a vertical two-strip:
two boxes are added in distinct rows.  Hence the number of nonzero rows never
decreases along a forward Pieri path.  Any path ending at a partition with at
most `N` rows therefore has every intermediate partition with at most `N`
rows.  Discarding states with more than `N` rows cannot remove a path
contributing to any final partition counted by `K_s(b)` or `B_s(b)`.  The
capped forward recurrence consequently gives the same coefficients for these
two sums as the uncapped recurrence.

## Lemma 16C.5 (CRT-certified row-capped D suffix replay)

Keep the notation of Lemma 16C.4.  Fix `s` and let `P` be a product of
pairwise coprime odd moduli.  Suppose the row-capped Pieri recurrence of
Lemma 16C.4 is replayed modulo each modulus and returns the residues of

```text
K_s(b) =
  sum_{nu partition s, ell(nu)<=2b} c_s(2nu_1,2nu_2,...)
```

and

```text
B_s(b) =
  sum_{nu partition s-b, ell(nu)<=2b}
      c_s(1+2nu_1,...,1+2nu_ell(nu),1^(2b-ell(nu))).
```

If

```text
P > m_s^stab
```

and

```text
P > binom(2b,2)^s,
```

then the CRT reconstruction from those residues gives the exact integers
`K_s(b)` and `B_s(b)`.  Consequently

```text
m_s^{D_b}=K_s(b)+B_s(b)
```

is exact.

Proof.  Lemma 16C.4 identifies `K_s(b)` and `B_s(b)` with sums of
coefficients produced by the row-capped vertical two-strip recurrence.  The
same recurrence over `Z/P_i Z` computes their residues modulo each factor
`P_i`, because the recurrence uses only addition of nonnegative integer
coefficients and reduction modulo `P_i` commutes with addition.

The stable moment is the sum of all even-row coefficients, and `K_s(b)` is a
subsum.  Thus

```text
0 <= K_s(b) <= m_s^stab.
```

At each row-capped Pieri step, from any state there are at most `binom(2b,2)`
choices of two distinct rows to receive the vertical two-strip.  Therefore the
total number of row-capped Pieri paths of length `s` is at most
`binom(2b,2)^s`, and `B_s(b)`, being a subcount of those paths, satisfies

```text
0 <= B_s(b) <= binom(2b,2)^s.
```

Since `P` is larger than each respective bound, each residue vector has a
unique representative in the indicated interval.  The Chinese remainder
theorem therefore reconstructs the exact values of `K_s(b)` and `B_s(b)`.
The final identity is Lemma 16C.4.

## Lemma 16C.6 (CRT-certified rank-four Weyl replay)

Let `G` be one of `B_4`, `C_4`, or `D_4`, and let `d_G` be the dimension of
its adjoint representation:

```text
d_{B_4}=36,        d_{C_4}=36,        d_{D_4}=28.
```

Fix `S>=0`.  Suppose the rank-four Weyl constant-term recurrence of
Proposition 17 is replayed modulo pairwise coprime odd primes whose product
is `P`, and suppose no prime divides the Weyl group order `|W_G|`.  If

```text
P > d_G^s        for every 0 <= s <= S,
```

then CRT reconstruction of the modular Weyl residues gives the exact moments
`m_s^G` for all `0<=s<=S`.  Consequently any integer polynomial expression
in those moments through `S`, including the Chain difference `D(m)` when
`2m+5<=S`, is exact after CRT reconstruction.

Proof.  Proposition 17 writes

```text
m_s^G = |W_G|^{-1} CT(chi_ad^s Delta_G),
```

where `Delta_G` is the Weyl density.  The modular replay uses the same
Laurent-polynomial recurrence for multiplication by `chi_ad` and the same
finite Weyl density.  Since each prime is coprime to `|W_G|`, multiplication
by `|W_G|^{-1}` is defined modulo that prime, so the replay returns the
residue of `m_s^G`.

The integral `m_s^G` is the multiplicity of the trivial representation in
`ad_G^{tensor s}`.  Hence

```text
0 <= m_s^G <= dim(ad_G^{tensor s}) = d_G^s.
```

The Chinese remainder theorem gives a unique representative in `[0,d_G^s]`
because the product modulus is larger than `d_G^s`.  Thus every reconstructed
`m_s^G` is the exact integer moment.  Substitution into an integer polynomial
expression then gives that expression exactly.

## Lemma 16D (finite B-boundary Pieri recurrence)

For `B_b=SO(2b+1)`, `b>=2`, put

```text
Gamma_j(b) = m_{2b+2+j}^{B_b} - m_{2b+2+j}^stab.
```

Let

```text
e_2^r = sum_lambda c_r(lambda) s_lambda
```

with the Pieri recurrence from Lemma 16C.  Then

```text
Gamma_j(b) =
  - sum_{nu partition 2b+2+j, ell(nu)>2b+1}
      c_{2b+2+j}(2nu_1,2nu_2,...).
```

Proof.  Let `N=2b+1` and `W=Lambda^2 R^N`.  The adjoint character of
`B_b` is `e_2`.  The `SO(N)` integral of this class function equals the
`O(N)` integral, as in Proposition 15.  Rains 1998, Section 3, gives the
orthogonal Schur integral criterion: the stable contribution consists of the
Schur summands with even row lengths, while the finite `O(N)` integral keeps
exactly those with at most `N` nonzero rows.

Thus the finite-minus-stable difference is exactly the negative sum of the
stable-contributing even-row Schur summands whose length exceeds `N`.  Writing
such a partition as `(2nu_1,2nu_2,...)` gives the displayed formula.  The
coefficients are computed by the vertical two-strip Pieri recurrence of
Lemma 16C.

## Lemma 16E (finite C-boundary Pieri recurrence)

For `C_b=Sp(b)`, `b>=2`, put

```text
Theta_j(b) = m_{2b+2+j}^{C_b} - m_{2b+2+j}^stab.
```

Let

```text
s_(2)^r = sum_lambda d_r(lambda) s_lambda.
```

Equivalently, if `e_2^r=sum_lambda c_r(lambda)s_lambda`, then
`d_r(lambda)=c_r(lambda')`, where `lambda'` is the conjugate partition.  Then

```text
Theta_j(b) =
  - sum_{mu partition 2b+2+j, ell(mu)>b}
      d_{2b+2+j}(mu_1,mu_1,...,mu_{ell(mu)},mu_{ell(mu)}).
```

Proof.  Proposition 15 identifies the `C_b` adjoint moment as
`int_{Sp(b)} s_(2)^r`.  Rains 1998, Section 3, gives the symplectic Schur
integral criterion: in the stable integral the contributing Schur summands are
exactly those whose row lengths occur with even multiplicity, while the
finite-rank `Sp(b)` integral keeps exactly those among them with at most `2b`
nonzero rows.

For `r=2b+2+j`, every Schur summand in `s_(2)^r` has at most `r` nonzero
rows.  A contributing summand omitted by finite rank is exactly one whose row
lengths occur with even multiplicity and whose length exceeds `2b`.  Writing
such a partition uniquely in the form

```text
(mu_1,mu_1,...,mu_{ell(mu)},mu_{ell(mu)})
```

gives a partition `mu` of `r` with `ell(mu)>b`.  These are present in the
stable symplectic integral and absent in the finite `Sp(b)` integral, giving
the displayed negative sum.

The equality `d_r(lambda)=c_r(lambda')` follows from the standard involution
on symmetric functions, which sends `h_2=s_(2)` to `e_2=s_(1,1)` and
`s_lambda` to `s_{lambda'}`.  The coefficients `c_r` are computed by the
vertical two-strip Pieri recurrence of Lemma 16C.

## Proposition 17 (Weyl torus formula for Q3)

Let `G` be a compact connected simple Lie group, let `T` be a maximal torus,
let `W` be the Weyl group, and choose positive roots `R_+`.  With normalized
Haar probability measure on `G` and on `T`, put

```text
Delta(t) = prod_{alpha in R_+} (exp(alpha(t)/2) - exp(-alpha(t)/2)).
```

Then, for every integer `n >= 0`,

```text
Q3^G(n)
  = |W|^(-2) int_{T x T}
      (chi_adj(s)-chi_adj(t))^2
      (chi_adj(s)+chi_adj(t))^n
      |Delta(s)|^2 |Delta(t)|^2 ds dt.
```

Proof.  Brocker-tom Dieck 1985, IV.(1.11), states Weyl's integral formula:
for every continuous class function `f` on `G`,

```text
int_G f(g) dg = |W|^(-1) int_T f(t) |Delta(t)|^2 dt.
```

The source text is present at
`/home/sky/yang/references/brocker_tomdieck_compact_lie.djvu`; text
extraction locates IV.(1.11) under "Weyl's Integral Formula" and gives the
compact connected group, maximal torus, Weyl group, and continuous-function
statement.  The determinant factor in IV.(1.11) restricts on a class function
to the displayed `|Delta|^2` root product.

The adjoint character is continuous and conjugation-invariant.  For fixed
`h`, the function

```text
g |-> (chi_adj(g)-chi_adj(h))^2 (chi_adj(g)+chi_adj(h))^n
```

is a continuous class function of `g`, so Weyl's formula applies to the first
variable.  For each resulting `s in T`, the same argument applies to the
second variable.  The two applications give the displayed `T x T` integral.
The normalization is checked by applying the same formula to `f=1`, which
gives `|W|^(-1) int_T |Delta(t)|^2 dt = 1`.

## Proposition 18 (Wong local Laplace expansion)

Let `D` be a domain in `R^d`, let `lambda > 0`, and set

```text
J(lambda) = int_D g(x) exp(-lambda f(x)) dx.
```

Assume:

```text
(i)  J(lambda) converges absolutely for all sufficiently large lambda;
(ii) for every epsilon > 0,
     inf { f(x)-f(x0) : x in D and |x-x0| >= epsilon } > 0;
(iii) x0 is an interior point of D and Hessian(f)(x0) is positive definite.
```

Then `J(lambda)` has an expansion

```text
J(lambda)
  ~ exp(-lambda f(x0)) sum_{k>=0} c_k lambda^(-d/2-k)
```

as `lambda -> infinity`.  If, after the Morse-coordinate change used in
Wong's proof,

```text
G(y) = g(h(y)) det h'(y)
```

has Taylor expansion whose first nonzero homogeneous term is `P_q(y)` of
degree `q`, then all earlier Taylor terms in Wong's coefficient formula vanish,
and the first possible nonzero term is the Gaussian integral of `P_q` against
`exp(-sum y_j^2/2)`, appearing at order
`lambda^(-d/2-q/2)` when `q` is even.  Odd homogeneous terms integrate to zero.

Moreover, on a fixed Morse-coordinate neighborhood, Taylor expansion through
degree `p-1` gives a remainder `delta_p(lambda)` satisfying

```text
|delta_p(lambda)| <= B_p lambda^(-(d+p)/2)
```

for all sufficiently large `lambda`, where `B_p` is obtained from the finite
suprema of the order-`p` derivatives of `G` on that neighborhood and the
corresponding Gaussian monomial integrals.

Proof.  Wong 2001, Chapter IX, section 5, p.494, introduces the Laplace-type
integral (5.1) and conditions (i)-(iii).  Theorem 3 on p.495 states exactly
that, when `x0` is an interior point and these conditions hold, (5.1) has the
displayed asymptotic expansion; the same theorem gives the leading
nonvanishing case `g(x0) != 0` as formula (5.3).

For the vanishing-amplitude refinement, Wong's proof applies the Morse lemma
near `x0` and obtains formula (5.5).  On p.496, Taylor's theorem is applied to
`G(y)=g(h(y))det h'(y)`, and termwise integration gives formula (5.6):
each Taylor monomial is integrated against the Gaussian
`exp(-sum y_j^2/2)`.  Therefore Taylor terms that are absent in `G` contribute
zero, and odd monomials integrate to zero by the one-dimensional parity
identity (5.8).  The first nonzero homogeneous even term gives the stated
first possible nonzero coefficient.  Wong's equation (5.7) writes the Taylor
remainder contribution explicitly, and equation (5.12) gives
`delta_p(lambda)=O(lambda^(-(d+p)/2))`.  The sentence immediately preceding
(5.12) explains the bound: the relevant derivatives `D^alpha G` are bounded
on the fixed coordinate neighborhood, and the remaining factors are Gaussian
monomial integrals.  Taking the maximum of those finitely many derivative
suprema gives the displayed constant `B_p`.

## Proposition 19 (Q3 saddle expansion at the identity)

Let `r = rank(G)`, `d = dim(g)`, and let `v,w` be real Lie-algebra
coordinates on the maximal torus near the identity.  Define

```text
Q_G(v) = sum_{alpha in R_+} alpha(v)^2.
```

Then `Q_G` is positive definite and

```text
chi_adj(exp(v)) = d - Q_G(v) + O(|v|^4),
|Delta(exp(v))|^2
  = prod_{alpha in R_+} alpha(v)^2 + O(|v|^(2|R_+|+2)).
```

Consequently, with

```text
phi(v,w) = log(chi_adj(exp(v)) + chi_adj(exp(w))),
g(v,w) = (chi_adj(exp(v))-chi_adj(exp(w)))^2
         |Delta(exp(v))|^2 |Delta(exp(w))|^2,
P(v,w) = (Q_G(w)-Q_G(v))^2
         prod_{alpha in R_+} alpha(v)^2 alpha(w)^2,
```

one has

```text
phi(v,w) = log(2d) - (Q_G(v)+Q_G(w))/(2d) + O(|(v,w)|^4),
g(v,w) = P(v,w) + O(|(v,w)|^(4|R_+|+6)).
```

The quadratic term of `phi` at `(0,0)` is negative definite.

Proof.  On the maximal torus,

```text
chi_adj(exp(v)) = r + sum_{alpha in R} exp(i alpha(v))
                = r + 2 sum_{alpha in R_+} cos(alpha(v)).
```

Using `2 cos(x)=2-x^2+O(x^4)` gives the displayed expansion for
`chi_adj`.  The positive roots span the dual Cartan space for a simple
root system, so `Q_G(v)=0` forces every root to vanish on `v`, hence
`v=0`; therefore `Q_G` is positive definite.

For the Weyl denominator,

```text
|exp(i alpha(v)/2)-exp(-i alpha(v)/2)|^2
  = 2 - 2 cos(alpha(v))
  = alpha(v)^2 + O(alpha(v)^4).
```

Multiplying over `R_+` gives the displayed expansion of `|Delta|^2`.
The expansion of `phi` follows by substituting

```text
chi_adj(exp(v)) + chi_adj(exp(w))
  = 2d - Q_G(v) - Q_G(w) + O(|(v,w)|^4)
```

into `log`.  Finally,

```text
chi_adj(exp(v)) - chi_adj(exp(w))
  = Q_G(w) - Q_G(v) + O(|(v,w)|^4),
```

so its square has leading term `(Q_G(w)-Q_G(v))^2` and additive remainder
of degree at least `6`.  Multiplication by the two denominator expansions
gives the displayed leading polynomial `P` and remainder degree
`4|R_+|+6`.  Since `Q_G` is positive definite, the quadratic part
`-(Q_G(v)+Q_G(w))/(2d)` of `phi` is negative definite.

## Proposition 20 (Gamma-Wronskian leading coefficient)

Let

```text
J_G = int_{R^r x R^r}
        (Q_G(y)-Q_G(x))^2
        prod_{alpha in R_+} alpha(x)^2 alpha(y)^2
        exp(-(Q_G(x)+Q_G(y))/(2d)) dx dy.
```

Then `J_G > 0`.  More explicitly, choose a linear change of variables
`x=L xi` with `Q_G(L xi)=|xi|^2`, and put

```text
P_L(xi) = prod_{alpha in R_+} alpha(L xi)^2,
sigma_G = int_{S^{r-1}} P_L(u) dS(u),
a_G = |R_+| + r/2,
lambda_G = 1/(2d).
```

Then `sigma_G > 0` and

```text
J_G = |det L|^2
      (sigma_G^2/2)
      a_G Gamma(a_G)^2
      lambda_G^(-2a_G-2).
```

Proof.  The positive definiteness of `Q_G` gives the change of variables.
After substituting `x=L xi`, `y=L eta`, the integral becomes `|det L|^2`
times

```text
int int (|eta|^2-|xi|^2)^2
        P_L(xi) P_L(eta)
        exp(-lambda_G(|xi|^2+|eta|^2)) dxi deta.
```

For `j=0,1,2`, define

```text
A_j = int_{R^r} |xi|^(2j) P_L(xi) exp(-lambda_G |xi|^2) dxi.
```

Expanding `(|eta|^2-|xi|^2)^2` gives

```text
J_G / |det L|^2 = 2(A_2 A_0 - A_1^2).
```

The polynomial `P_L` is homogeneous of degree `2|R_+|`.  In polar
coordinates,

```text
A_j = sigma_G int_0^infty rho^(2j+2|R_+|+r-1)
                  exp(-lambda_G rho^2) d rho
    = (sigma_G/2) lambda_G^(-a_G-j) Gamma(a_G+j).
```

The angular integral `sigma_G` is positive: `P_L(u) >= 0`, and its zero
set on the sphere is contained in the finite union of root hyperplane
sections, which has spherical measure zero.

Therefore

```text
2(A_2 A_0 - A_1^2)
  = (sigma_G^2/2) lambda_G^(-2a_G-2)
    (Gamma(a_G+2)Gamma(a_G) - Gamma(a_G+1)^2).
```

Using `Gamma(a_G+1)=a_G Gamma(a_G)` and
`Gamma(a_G+2)=(a_G+1)a_G Gamma(a_G)`, the parenthesis equals
`a_G Gamma(a_G)^2`.  All factors in the displayed formula for `J_G` are
positive.

## Proposition 21 (negative-region bound)

Let `d=dim(g)` and

```text
C_G = max_{g in G} (-chi_adj(g)).
```

In the torus formula of Proposition 17, let `I_-(n)` be the contribution
from

```text
T_- = { (s,t) in T x T : chi_adj(s)+chi_adj(t) < 0 }.
```

Then, for every integer `n >= 0`,

```text
|I_-(n)| <= 4 d^2 (2 C_G)^n.
```

Proof.  For every unitary representative of the adjoint representation,
`|chi_adj(g)| <= d`, because the character is the trace of a `d x d`
unitary matrix.  Hence

```text
|chi_adj(s)-chi_adj(t)| <= 2d.
```

On `T_-`, each adjoint-character value is at least `-C_G`, and the sum is
negative.  Therefore

```text
|chi_adj(s)+chi_adj(t)|
  = -(chi_adj(s)+chi_adj(t)) <= 2 C_G.
```

Taking absolute values in the `T_-` part of Proposition 17 gives the pointwise
bound

```text
|(chi_adj(s)-chi_adj(t))^2(chi_adj(s)+chi_adj(t))^n|
  <= 4 d^2 (2C_G)^n.
```

The Weyl-density factor has total normalized mass one:

```text
|W|^(-2) int_{T x T} |Delta(s)|^2 |Delta(t)|^2 ds dt = 1,
```

by Proposition 17 applied to the constant class function `1` in each
variable.  Integrating the pointwise bound over `T_-` gives the displayed
estimate.

## Proposition 22 (central saddle set and secondary-region bound)

Let `Z_T = Z(G) cap T`, and let `U` be an open neighborhood of `Z_T` in
`T`.  Define

```text
eta_G(U) = d - max_{t in T \ U} chi_adj(t).
```

Then `eta_G(U) > 0`.  If `I_{+,sec,U}(n)` denotes the contribution in the
Weyl formula of Proposition 17 from

```text
T_{+,sec,U}
  = { (s,t) in T x T :
        chi_adj(s)+chi_adj(t) >= 0 and (s,t) notin U x U },
```

then, for every integer `n >= 0`,

```text
|I_{+,sec,U}(n)| <= 4 d^2 (2d - eta_G(U))^n.
```

Moreover, for each pair `(z_1,z_2) in Z_T x Z_T`, the local saddle
expansion at `(z_1,z_2)` is the translate of the identity-saddle expansion in
Propositions 19-20.  In particular each central saddle pair has the same
positive leading Gaussian coefficient `J_G`.

Proof.  The adjoint representation may be represented by orthogonal, hence
unitary, matrices of dimension `d`.  Therefore

```text
chi_adj(g) <= d
```

for every `g in G`.  Equality holds exactly when every eigenvalue of
`Ad(g)` is `1`, hence exactly when `Ad(g)=1`, equivalently when
`g in Z(G)`.

Since `G` has simple Lie algebra, `Z(G)` has zero Lie algebra.  Thus `Z(G)`
is compact and discrete, hence finite.  The equality set of `chi_adj` on
`T` is therefore the finite set `Z_T`.  For the compact set `T \ U`, the
maximum of `chi_adj` is strictly below `d`, so `eta_G(U)>0`.

On `T_{+,sec,U}`, at least one of `s,t` lies outside `U`.  The previous
paragraph gives

```text
chi_adj(s)+chi_adj(t) <= 2d - eta_G(U).
```

Since this region also has nonnegative character sum,

```text
|chi_adj(s)+chi_adj(t)| <= 2d - eta_G(U).
```

As in Proposition 21,

```text
|chi_adj(s)-chi_adj(t)| <= 2d.
```

and the total normalized Weyl-density mass on `T x T` is one.  Taking
absolute values in Proposition 17 over `T_{+,sec,U}` gives the displayed
bound.

Finally, if `z in Z_T`, then `Ad(z)=1`.  Hence

```text
chi_adj(z exp v) = chi_adj(exp v).
```

Every root is trivial on `Z_T`, so also

```text
Delta(z exp v) = Delta(exp v)
```

up to the same Weyl-denominator convention used in Proposition 17; in
absolute square it is identical.  Translating Haar measure by central
elements preserves the local coordinate measure.  Thus the Taylor expansion,
vanishing order, and Gaussian coefficient at `(z_1,z_2)` are exactly those
computed at `(1,1)` in Propositions 19-20.

## Proposition 23 (negative base is strictly subleading)

For the constant `C_G` in Proposition 21,

```text
C_G < d.
```

Consequently the negative-region estimate in Proposition 21 has exponential
base strictly smaller than the central positive-saddle base `2d`:

```text
2 C_G < 2d.
```

Proof.  Proposition 21 already gives `chi_adj(g) >= -d`, so `C_G <= d`.
Assume for contradiction that `C_G=d`.  By compactness, there is `g in G`
with `chi_adj(g)=-d`.  The eigenvalues of the unitary matrix `Ad(g)` all
have real part at least `-1`, and their real parts sum to `-d`; hence every
eigenvalue is `-1`, so `Ad(g)=-1` on `Lie(G)`.

The map `Ad(g)` is a Lie algebra automorphism.  If `Ad(g)=-1`, then for all
`X,Y in Lie(G)`,

```text
Ad(g)[X,Y] = -[X,Y],
[Ad(g)X, Ad(g)Y] = [-X,-Y] = [X,Y].
```

Automorphism compatibility gives `-[X,Y]=[X,Y]`, hence `[X,Y]=0` for all
`X,Y`.  This contradicts the nonabelian simple Lie algebra condition.
Therefore `C_G<d`.

## Proposition 24 (Wong tail threshold for the positive saddle)

Let `G` be one of the remaining classical families in Corollary 4.  Put

```text
alpha_G = rank(G) + 2|R_+| + 2.
```

Choose exponential coordinates `v` on `T` near the identity, using the same
Lebesgue measure as in Propositions 19-20.  Let `kappa_T > 0` be the local
Haar-density constant, so that normalized Haar measure on `T` is
`kappa_T dv` in these coordinates.  Define

```text
A_G = |Z_T|^2 |W|^(-2) kappa_T^2 J_G.
```

Then `A_G > 0`.  Let `N_W(G)` and `B_G` be the finite Wong cutoff and
remainder constant obtained from Proposition 18, with Taylor order
`4|R_+|+6`, applied to the disjoint central coordinate neighborhoods from
Proposition 22.  Thus the central positive-saddle contribution `I_cent(n)`
satisfies

```text
|I_cent(n) - A_G (2d)^n n^(-alpha_G)|
  <= B_G (2d)^n n^(-alpha_G-1)
```

for every integer `n >= N_W(G)`.

Let

```text
N_loc(G) = ceil(max(N_W(G), 1, 2B_G/A_G)),
rho_G = C_G/d,
L_G = log(d/C_G),
M_G = 8d^2/A_G,
S_G = max(0, alpha_G log(2 alpha_G/(exp(1) L_G))),
N_tail(G)
  = ceil(max(N_loc(G), 2(max(log(M_G),0)+S_G)/L_G)).
```

Then, for every odd integer `n >= N_tail(G)`,

```text
Q3^G(n) >= 0.
```

For even `n`, `Q3^G(n) >= 0` pointwise.

Proof.  Proposition 20 gives `J_G>0`, while `|Z_T|`, `|W|`, and `kappa_T`
are positive finite constants.  Therefore `A_G>0`.

The dimension of the local torus-pair coordinate space is `2 rank(G)`.  By
Proposition 19, the amplitude has first nonzero homogeneous term of degree

```text
4|R_+| + 4.
```

Therefore Proposition 18 places the first nonzero local coefficient at order

```text
rank(G) + 2|R_+| + 2 = alpha_G.
```

The coefficient at each central saddle pair is the same by Proposition 22,
and Proposition 20 identifies it as `|W|^(-2) kappa_T^2 J_G`.  Summing over
`Z_T x Z_T` gives the displayed leading coefficient `A_G`.  The quantitative
remainder part of Proposition 18, applied one Taylor degree later, gives the
displayed bound with the finite constant `B_G`.

For `n >= N_loc(G)`,

```text
I_cent(n) >= (A_G/2) (2d)^n n^(-alpha_G).
```

For odd `n`, split the Weyl integral into the nonnegative positive-sum region
and the negative-sum region.  The positive-sum region contains
`I_cent(n)`, while Proposition 21 gives

```text
|I_-(n)| <= 4 d^2 (2C_G)^n.
```

It remains to compare these two terms.  Since Proposition 23 gives
`C_G<d`, we have `rho_G in (0,1)` and `L_G=log(d/C_G)>0`.

For every `x>0` and `a>0`,

```text
log(x) <= a x - 1 - log(a).
```

Apply this with `a=L_G/(2 alpha_G)` and `x=n`.  This gives

```text
alpha_G log(n)
  <= (L_G/2)n + alpha_G log(2 alpha_G/(exp(1)L_G)).
```

By the definition of `S_G`, every `n >= N_tail(G)` satisfies

```text
n L_G - alpha_G log(n) >= log(M_G).
```

Equivalently,

```text
rho_G^n n^(alpha_G) <= A_G/(8d^2).
```

Hence

```text
4d^2 (2C_G)^n
  <= (A_G/2) (2d)^n n^(-alpha_G)
  <= I_cent(n).
```

Thus the central positive-saddle contribution dominates the possible negative
contribution, and `Q3^G(n) >= 0` for odd `n >= N_tail(G)`.  If `n` is even,
the original integrand `(chi(g)-chi(h))^2(chi(g)+chi(h))^n` is pointwise
nonnegative, so the Haar integral is nonnegative.

## Lemma 24.1 (classical root-data constants for the tail)

For the remaining classical root systems, indexed by rank `b`, the constants
entering Proposition 24 are:

```text
family  group        d=dim(G)     |R_+|      alpha_G        |W|
B_b     SO(2b+1)     b(2b+1)      b^2        2b^2+b+2       2^b b!
C_b     Sp(b)        b(2b+1)      b^2        2b^2+b+2       2^b b!
D_b     SO(2b)       b(2b-1)      b(b-1)     2b^2-b+2       2^(b-1) b!
```

Proof.  The root systems have the standard descriptions

```text
B_b:  {+-e_i} union {+-e_i+-e_j : i<j},
C_b:  {+-2e_i} union {+-e_i+-e_j : i<j},
D_b:  {+-e_i+-e_j : i<j}.
```

Therefore the positive-root counts are

```text
|R_+(B_b)| = b + 2 binom(b,2) = b^2,
|R_+(C_b)| = b + 2 binom(b,2) = b^2,
|R_+(D_b)| = 2 binom(b,2) = b(b-1).
```

The Lie algebra dimension is `rank + 2|R_+|`, giving

```text
dim(B_b)=b+2b^2=b(2b+1),
dim(C_b)=b+2b^2=b(2b+1),
dim(D_b)=b+2b(b-1)=b(2b-1).
```

Proposition 24 defines `alpha_G=rank(G)+2|R_+|+2`, so the displayed
`alpha_G` values follow.  For the Weyl-group orders, Brocker-tom Dieck
IV.(3.6) identifies the Weyl group of `SO(2b+1)` with the signed permutation
group and the Weyl group of `SO(2b)` with its even-signed subgroup; IV.(3.8)
identifies the Weyl group of `Sp(b)` with the signed permutation group.
Therefore the orders are `2^b b!`, `2^(b-1)b!`, and `2^b b!`, respectively.

## Lemma 24.2 (classical adjoint-character negative bases)

Let `C_G=max_{g in G}(-chi_adj(g))` be the constant from Proposition 21.  For
the remaining classical families,

```text
B_b: C_G = b,
C_b: C_G = b,
D_b: C_G = b      if b is even,
D_b: C_G = b-2    if b is odd.
```

Proof.  Brocker-tom Dieck IV.(1.6), the main theorem on tori, states that
every element of a compact connected Lie group is contained in a maximal
torus, and all maximal tori are conjugate.  Since `chi_adj` is a class
function, it is enough to minimize it on the standard maximal torus.  Let
`T_j` denote the trace of `g^j` in the defining representation.

For `B_b=SO(2b+1)`, the defining eigenvalues are

```text
1, z_1, z_1^(-1), ..., z_b, z_b^(-1),
```

with `z_j=exp(i theta_j)`.  Put `x_j=cos(theta_j)`,
`S=sum_j x_j`, and `Q=sum_j x_j^2`.  Proposition 5 gives
`chi_adj=(T_1^2-T_2)/2`, so

```text
chi_adj = (T_1^2 - T_2)/2
        = b + 2S + 2(S^2-Q)
        = b + 2S + 4 sum_{i<j} x_i x_j.
```

This expression is affine in each variable separately, so its minimum on the
cube `[-1,1]^b` occurs at a vertex.  At a vertex `x_j=+-1`, `Q=b` and
`S=b-2k` for some integer `k`, hence

```text
chi_adj = 2S^2 + 2S - b.
```

If `b` is even, the minimum is attained at `S=0` and equals `-b`.  If `b` is
odd, the minimum is attained at `S=-1` and also equals `-b`.  Thus `C_G=b`
for `B_b`.

For `D_b=SO(2b)`, the defining eigenvalues are

```text
z_1, z_1^(-1), ..., z_b, z_b^(-1).
```

Again using Proposition 5, one obtains

```text
chi_adj = b + 2(S^2-Q)
        = b + 4 sum_{i<j} x_i x_j.
```

This is also affine in each `x_j`, so the minimum occurs at a vertex.  There
`Q=b` and

```text
chi_adj = 2S^2 - b.
```

If `b` is even, `S=0` is allowed and the minimum is `-b`.  If `b` is odd, the
nearest allowed values are `S=+-1`, and the minimum is `2-b`.  Therefore
`C_G=b` for even `b` and `C_G=b-2` for odd `b`.

For `C_b=Sp(b)`, the defining eigenvalues are the same torus list as in
`D_b`.  Proposition 5 gives `chi_adj=(T_1^2+T_2)/2`, hence

```text
chi_adj = (T_1^2 + T_2)/2
        = 2S^2 + 2Q - b.
```

Since `S^2>=0` and `Q>=0`, this is at least `-b`.  Equality is attained at
`x_1=...=x_b=0`, equivalently `theta_j=pi/2` for every `j`.  Thus `C_G=b`
for `C_b`.

## Corollary 24.3 (classical tail threshold after root-data insertion)

After Lemmas 24.1 and 24.2, the exponential part of Proposition 24 is explicit
for each remaining classical family:

```text
B_b,C_b: rho_G = 1/(2b+1),          L_G = log(2b+1),
D_b, b even: rho_G = 1/(2b-1),      L_G = log(2b-1),
D_b, b odd:  rho_G = (b-2)/(b(2b-1)),
              L_G = log(b(2b-1)/(b-2)).
```

The power `alpha_G` and Weyl denominator `|W|` are the values in Lemma 24.1.
Thus the only family constants in `N_tail(G)` not yet numerically calibrated
are the local positive-saddle constants `A_G`, `B_G`, and `N_W(G)` from
Propositions 18-20.

Proof.  Substitute the dimensions from Lemma 24.1 and the values of `C_G`
from Lemma 24.2 into `rho_G=C_G/d` and `L_G=log(d/C_G)` in Proposition 24.

## Lemma 24.4 (the current Wong cutoff leaves a high-rank middle window)

Let `N_suf(G)` denote the explicit sufficient cutoff supplied by Proposition
24, so that

```text
N_suf(G) >= 2 S_G/L_G.
```

The stable Chain window from Corollary 16 reaches at most

```text
B_b,C_b: odd n <= 2b-1,
D_b:     odd n <= b-3.
```

For the sufficient cutoff in Proposition 24,

```text
(2S_G/L_G)/(2b-1) -> infinity       for B_b and C_b,
(2S_G/L_G)/(b-3)  -> infinity       for D_b.
```

Consequently, the combination of Corollary 16, Proposition 24, and any fixed
finite exact bridge depth leaves an unbounded high-rank middle window.  Closing
`CMO-ClassicalTail` by this route therefore requires either a sharper tail
threshold than Proposition 24 or a separate middle-rank Chain theorem.

Proof.  For `B_b` and `C_b`, Corollary 16 proves Chain step `r` in rank `b`
when `b >= r+2`.  Thus stable Chain propagation reaches odd
`n=2r+3` only through `n <= 2b-1`.  For `D_b`, Corollary 16 requires
`b >= 2r+6`, so it reaches no further than `n <= b-3`.

By Corollary 24.3,

```text
B_b,C_b: alpha_G = 2b^2+b+2,      L_G = log(2b+1),
D_b:     alpha_G = 2b^2-b+2,      L_G ~ log(2b).
```

In every one of the three families,

```text
alpha_G ~ 2b^2,
L_G ~ log b,
log(2 alpha_G/(exp(1)L_G)) ~ 2 log b.
```

Hence

```text
S_G = alpha_G log(2 alpha_G/(exp(1)L_G)) ~ 4b^2 log b,
2S_G/L_G ~ 8b^2.
```

Dividing by the stable Chain reaches `2b-1` for `B_b,C_b` and `b-3` for
`D_b` gives quantities tending to infinity.

If an exact bridge is fixed through some `m=M`, it reaches only odd
`n <= 2M+3`.  Taking `b` larger than `M` and then using the preceding
asymptotic leaves odd integers above both the fixed bridge and the stable
window but below the sufficient tail cutoff.  Thus fixed-depth bridge
extension cannot discharge the remaining assumption when paired only with the
current Proposition 24 cutoff.

## Proposition 24.5 (classical Macdonald-Mehta leading coefficient)

Use the standard Euclidean normalization in which long roots have squared
length `2`, and write

```text
sum_{alpha in R_+} alpha(x)^2 = kappa_G |x|^2.
```

For the remaining classical families,

```text
family  kappa_G    degrees d_i                 z_G
B_b     2b-1       2,4,...,2b                  1
C_b     b+1        2,4,...,2b                  2
D_b     2b-2       2,4,...,2b-2,b              2
```

where `z_G=|Z(G) cap T|` for the displayed compact model.  With
`a_G=|R_+|+r/2=d/2`, the leading coefficient from Proposition 24 is

```text
A_G =
  z_G^2 * 8 a_G d^2 (d/kappa_G)^d
  * prod_i ((d_i-1)!)^2 / (2 pi)^r.
```

Proof.  Lemma 24.1 gives the root systems and the Weyl-group degrees.  The
displayed `kappa_G` values follow by summing root squared lengths:

```text
B_b: b*1 + b(b-1)*2 = b(2b-1),
C_b: b*2 + b(b-1)*1 = b(b+1),
D_b: b(b-1)*2       = b(2b-2).
```

The center sizes are immediate for the displayed groups:
`SO(2b+1)` has trivial center, while `Sp(b)` and `SO(2b)` have center
`{+-I}` inside the standard maximal torus.

Etingof 2009, Theorem 4.1, the Macdonald-Mehta integral for finite Coxeter
groups, gives at exponent `1`

```text
int exp(-|y|^2/2) prod_{alpha in R_+} alpha(y)^2 dy
  = (2pi)^(r/2) prod_i d_i!.
```

After the scaling `y=(kappa_G/d)^(1/2) x`,

```text
Z_G := int prod_alpha alpha(x)^2 exp(-Q_G(x)/(2d)) dx
     = (d/kappa_G)^(d/2) (2pi)^(r/2) prod_i d_i!.
```

For the Gamma-Wronskian integral of Proposition 20, radial integration gives

```text
J_G = 8 a_G d^2 Z_G^2.
```

Indeed, with respect to the density proportional to
`prod_alpha alpha(x)^2 exp(-Q_G(x)/(2d))`, the variable `Q_G(x)` has gamma
shape `a_G` and rate `1/(2d)`, so

```text
int int (Q_G(y)-Q_G(x))^2 ... dx dy
  = 2 Z_G^2 Var(Q_G)
  = 8 a_G d^2 Z_G^2.
```

Finally substitute this into
`A_G=z_G^2 |W|^(-2) (2pi)^(-2r) J_G`, using
`|W|=prod_i d_i`.  The displayed formula follows.

## Lemma 24.6 (leading-coefficient dominance after the stable edge)

Let `n_st(G)` be the last odd exponent reached by Corollary 16, with the
base value `n=1` included in the `D_4` endpoint:

```text
B_b,C_b: n_st=2b-1,
D_b:     n_st=b-3.
```

Define the leading-term dominance margin for odd `n >= n_st(G)` by

```text
M_lead(G,n)
  = log(A_G) + n log(d/C_G) - alpha_G log(n) - log(8d^2).
```

Then

```text
min_{odd n >= n_st(G)} M_lead(G,n) -> +infinity
```

in each of the families `B_b`, `C_b`, and `D_b`.

Proof.  By Proposition 24.5 and Stirling's formula,

```text
log A_G = 4b^2 log b + O(b^2)
```

in each of the three families.  Lemmas 24.1 and 24.2 give
`alpha_G=2b^2+O(b)` and `log(d/C_G)=log b+O(1)`.  The continuous function

```text
n log(d/C_G) - alpha_G log(n)
```

has its minimum at

```text
n = alpha_G/log(d/C_G).
```

At this minimum,

```text
alpha_G - alpha_G log(alpha_G/log(d/C_G))
  = -4b^2 log b + 2b^2 log log b + O(b^2),
log(8d^2)=O(log b).
```

Substitution gives

```text
min_{n >= n_st(G)} M_lead(G,n)
  = 2b^2 log log b + O(b^2),
```

which tends to `+infinity`.

The exact checker `character_ring_iter/classical_tail_constants.py` evaluates
the same expression with the rational lower bound `pi<4`, including the
post-stable minimum over odd `n`.  For ranks through `12`, the smallest
displayed lower-bound minimum is already positive:

```text
B_2:  min log10 margin >= 0.43,
C_2:  min log10 margin >= 1.03,
D_4:  min log10 margin >= 1.53.
```

Thus the high-rank obstruction in Lemma 24.4 is not the algebraic size of the
formal leading coefficient.  The remaining question is whether a rank-growing
tail theorem can make enough of that coefficient available in the
post-stable range.

## Lemma 24.7 (fixed local caps do not give stable-edge half-leading)

Fix a root-angle cap `delta > 0`.  In the identity-saddle scaling
`v=x/sqrt(n)`, the cap

```text
|alpha(v)| <= delta       for every root alpha
```

is contained in the radial event

```text
S := Q_G(x)/(2d) <= |R_+| delta^2 n/(2d),
```

where `Q_G(x)=sum_{alpha in R_+} alpha(x)^2`.  Let
`a_G=d/2`.  At the stable edge `n=n_st(G)` of Lemma 24.6,

```text
|R_+| delta^2 n_st(G)/(2d a_G) -> 0
```

in each of the families `B_b`, `C_b`, and `D_b`.  Consequently the fraction of
the Macdonald-Mehta Gamma-Wronskian leading coefficient lying in any fixed
root-angle cap tends to `0` at the stable edge.

More quantitatively, if `X` and `Y` are independent gamma variables with
shape `a_G` and rate `1`, and

```text
S_cap = |R_+| delta^2 n_st(G)/(2d),
```

then the capped Wronskian fraction is bounded by

```text
E[(X-Y)^2 1_{X<=S_cap} 1_{Y<=S_cap}] / E[(X-Y)^2]
 <= (S_cap^2/a_G) (e S_cap/a_G)^(2a_G).
```

In particular, a proof that uses only a fixed root-angle Taylor cap cannot
yield a fixed fraction, such as `A_G/2`, of the full Macdonald-Mehta leading
coefficient at `n=n_st(G)`.

Proof.  If the root-angle cap holds, then for every positive root

```text
alpha(x)^2 <= delta^2 n.
```

Summing this inequality over `R_+` gives

```text
Q_G(x)=sum_{alpha in R_+} alpha(x)^2 <= |R_+| delta^2 n.
```

Dividing by `2d` gives the displayed radial event containing the cap.

The Macdonald-Mehta radial variable `S=Q_G(x)/(2d)` has gamma shape
`a_G=d/2` and rate `1`.  The Gamma-Wronskian coefficient is the expectation
of `(Y-X)^2` against two independent copies of this radial law, and
`E[(Y-X)^2]=2a_G`.

On the event `X,Y <= S_cap`, one has `(X-Y)^2 <= S_cap^2`, hence

```text
E[(X-Y)^2 1_{X<=S_cap}1_{Y<=S_cap}] / E[(X-Y)^2]
 <= (S_cap^2/a_G) P(X<=S_cap)^2.
```

For `S_cap<a_G`, the standard Chernoff lower-tail bound for a gamma variable
gives

```text
P(X<=S_cap) <= (e S_cap/a_G)^a_G.
```

Substitution gives the displayed upper bound.

It remains to evaluate `S_cap/a_G` at the stable edge.  Lemmas 24.1 and 24.6
give

```text
B_b: d=b(2b+1), |R_+|=b^2,    n_st=2b-1,
C_b: d=b(2b+1), |R_+|=b^2,    n_st=2b-1,
D_b: d=b(2b-1), |R_+|=b(b-1), n_st=b-3.
```

Since `a_G=d/2`, in all three cases

```text
S_cap/a_G = |R_+| delta^2 n_st(G)/d^2 = O_delta(b^(-1)).
```

The Chernoff bound therefore tends to `0`.
The diagnostic command

```text
python3 -u ginibre_q3/character_ring_iter/classical_tail_constants.py \
  --max-rank 50 --cap-diagnostic
```

prints the same quantity.  With the maximal fixed cap `delta=pi`, the
rank-50 upper bounds for the capped Wronskian fraction are already at most
`10^(-2949.92)` for `B_50`, `10^(-2949.92)` for `C_50`, and
`10^(-4451.07)` for `D_50`.

## Proposition 24.8 (nonlocal pushforward tail criteria)

Let `G` be any compact connected simple group in the remaining classical
branch, and write `X=chi_adj(g)`, `Y=chi_adj(h)` for independent Haar
variables.  Let `psi_G` be the positive finite measure on the real line
defined by

```text
int f(s) d psi_G(s)
  = E[(X-Y)^2 f(X+Y)].
```

Then

```text
Q3^G(n) = int s^n d psi_G(s)
```

for every integer `n >= 0`, and

```text
psi_G(R) = 2.
```

Let `C_G=max(-chi_adj)` as in Proposition 21.  For any `c>2C_G`, put

```text
P_G(c) = psi_G([c,2d]).
```

Then, for every odd integer `n >= 1`,

```text
Q3^G(n) >= c^n P_G(c) - 2(2C_G)^n.
```

Consequently, if

```text
P_G(c) >= 2 (2C_G/c)^n,
```

then `Q3^G(n) >= 0`; if this inequality holds at an odd `n_0`, then it holds
for every odd `n >= n_0`.

More generally, for `0<a<2C_G<c`, put

```text
N_G(a) = psi_G([-2C_G,-a]).
```

Then, for every odd integer `n >= 1`,

```text
Q3^G(n) >= c^n P_G(c) - (2C_G)^n N_G(a) - 2 a^n.
```

Consequently, if

```text
P_G(c) >= N_G(a) (2C_G/c)^n + 2 (a/c)^n,
```

then `Q3^G(n) >= 0`; if this inequality holds at an odd `n_0`, then it holds
for every odd `n >= n_0`.

Proof.  The first identity is Proposition 8 written at the level of the
random variables `X` and `Y`, or equivalently Proposition 17 after pushing
forward by `(s,t) |-> chi_adj(s)+chi_adj(t)`.

The adjoint representation of a compact connected simple group is
irreducible over `C`, has no invariant vector, and has real character.
Schur orthogonality gives

```text
E[X]=0,       E[X^2]=1.
```

Since `X` and `Y` are independent with the same distribution,

```text
psi_G(R)=E[(X-Y)^2]=2E[X^2]-2E[X]E[Y]=2.
```

For odd `n`, split the pushforward integral into `s>=c`, `0<=s<c`, and
`s<0`.  The middle part is nonnegative.  On `s>=c`, the contribution is at
least `c^n P_G(c)`.  On `s<0`, Proposition 21 gives `s>=-2C_G`; hence
`s^n >= -(2C_G)^n`.  The total `psi_G`-mass of the negative half-line is at
most `psi_G(R)=2`.  Therefore

```text
int_{s<0} s^n d psi_G(s) >= -2(2C_G)^n.
```

Adding the three pieces gives the displayed lower bound.  The two
consequences are immediate after division by `(2C_G)^n`: the right side is
nonnegative when `P_G(c)(c/(2C_G))^n >= 2`, and the left factor
`(c/(2C_G))^n` increases with odd `n` because `c>2C_G`.

For the sharper estimate, split the negative half-line into `s in [-2C_G,-a]`
and `s in [-a,0]`.  On the first piece, `s^n >= -(2C_G)^n`; on the second,
`s^n >= -a^n`.  The second piece has `psi_G`-mass at most `2`, while the
first has mass `N_G(a)`.  This gives the displayed two-sided lower bound.
After division by `c^n`, the condition

```text
P_G(c) >= N_G(a)(2C_G/c)^n + 2(a/c)^n
```

is exactly the nonnegativity of that lower bound.  For fixed `a,c`, the two
right-hand factors decrease as odd `n` increases because `a<c` and
`2C_G<c`, so verification at `n_0` propagates to all later odd exponents.

## Corollary 24.9 (stable-edge two-sided pushforward target)

For a remaining classical family and rank, choose any `0<a_G<2C_G<c_G`.  If

```text
P_G(c_G) >= N_G(a_G) (2C_G/c_G)^(n_st(G)) + 2 (a_G/c_G)^(n_st(G)),
```

where `n_st(G)` is the stable edge from Lemma 24.6, then the odd tail
`Q3^G(n) >= 0` holds for every odd `n >= n_st(G)`.

Thus the high-rank part of `CMO-ClassicalTail` is reduced to the single
nonlocal comparison above between the positive pushforward tail
`P_G(c_G)` and the negative near-edge pushforward tail `N_G(a_G)`.  Unlike the
fixed local-cap route of Lemma 24.7, this target may use all configurations
with `chi_adj(g)+chi_adj(h) >= c_G` and all cancellations showing that the
negative pushforward mass near `-2C_G` is small.

Proof.  Apply Proposition 24.8 at `n=n_st(G)` and then use its monotonicity in
odd `n`.

## Proposition 24.10 (pushforward tails reduce to defining-trace deviations)

Let `G` be one of `B_b`, `C_b`, or `D_b`, and let `U` be Haar-distributed in
the defining representation.  Put

```text
T_1(U)=Tr(U),     T_2(U)=Tr(U^2).
```

Define

```text
tau_G(U)=T_2(U)    for B_b,D_b,
tau_G(U)=-T_2(U)   for C_b.
```

For `1<a<2C_G<c`, set

```text
p_1(G;c,a) = Pr(|T_1(U)| >= sqrt(2c+3a)),
p_2(G;a)   = Pr(tau_G(U) >= a).
```

Then

```text
P_G(c) >= c^2 (1-a^(-2)) (p_1(G;c,a)-p_2(G;a)),
N_G(a) <= 2(C_G^2+1) p_2(G;a).
```

Consequently the stable-edge tail in Corollary 24.9 follows from the
defining-trace inequality

```text
c^2 (1-a^(-2)) (p_1(G;c,a)-p_2(G;a))
  >= 2(C_G^2+1) p_2(G;a) (2C_G/c)^(n_st(G))
     + 2(a/c)^(n_st(G)).
```

Proof.  Proposition 5 gives the adjoint character formulas

```text
B_b,D_b: chi_adj(U) = (T_1(U)^2 - T_2(U))/2,
C_b:     chi_adj(U) = (T_1(U)^2 + T_2(U))/2.
```

On the event

```text
|T_1(U)| >= sqrt(2c+3a),       tau_G(U) <= a,
```

the adjoint character satisfies `chi_adj(U) >= c+a`.  Indeed, in the
`B_b,D_b` cases, `tau_G=T_2`, so

```text
chi_adj(U) = (T_1(U)^2 - T_2(U))/2
            >= (2c+3a-a)/2 = c+a.
```

In the `C_b` case, `tau_G=-T_2`, so `T_2(U)>=-a`, and

```text
chi_adj(U) = (T_1(U)^2 + T_2(U))/2
            >= (2c+3a-a)/2 = c+a.
```

Let `X=chi_adj(U)` and let `Y` be an independent copy.  On the event
`|T_1(U)| >= sqrt(2c+3a)`, `tau_G(U)<=a`, and `|Y| <= a`, one has
`X >= c+a`, whence `X+Y >= c` and `|X-Y| >= c`.  Thus this event contributes
at least `c^2` to the weighted pushforward mass `P_G(c)`.  Its first two
conditions have probability at least `p_1(G;c,a)-p_2(G;a)`.  Schur
orthogonality gives `E[Y^2]=1`, so Chebyshev gives

```text
Pr(|Y|<=a) >= 1-a^(-2).
```

Independence proves

```text
P_G(c) >= c^2 (1-a^(-2)) (p_1(G;c,a)-p_2(G;a)).
```

For the negative tail, if `X+Y <= -a`, then either `X <= -a/2` or
`Y <= -a/2`.  By symmetry and independence,

```text
N_G(a)
 <= 2 E[(X-Y)^2 1_{X<=-a/2}]
  = 2(E[X^2 1_{X<=-a/2}] + Pr(X<=-a/2) E[Y^2]),
```

because `E[Y]=0`.  On `X<=-a/2`, Proposition 21 gives `-C_G<=X<0`, so
`X^2<=C_G^2`; Schur orthogonality gives `E[Y^2]=1`.  Therefore

```text
N_G(a) <= 2(C_G^2+1) Pr(X <= -a/2).
```

In the `B_b,D_b` cases, `X <= -a/2` implies

```text
T_2(U) >= T_1(U)^2 + a >= a.
```

In the `C_b` case, `X <= -a/2` implies

```text
-T_2(U) >= T_1(U)^2 + a >= a.
```

Thus `Pr(X <= -a/2) <= p_2(G;a)`, proving the displayed bound for `N_G(a)`.
Substituting the two estimates into Corollary 24.9 gives the final
defining-trace inequality.

## Corollary 24.11 (parameterized high-rank trace-deviation target)

Fix constants `r>2`, `0<eta<1`, `A>0`, and `K>0` with

```text
A < log(r/eta).
```

Let `C_0` be an integer with `C_0>eta^(-1)` such that, for every real
`C>=C_0`,

```text
R_0(C) := exp(A C - K C log C) <= 1/2,

R_1(C) :=
  [4(1+C^(-2))(r/2)^3/(r^2(1-eta^(-2) C^(-2)))]
  exp(A C - K C log C - C log(r/2)) <= 1/2,

R_2(C) :=
  [4(r/eta)^3/(r^2 C^2(1-eta^(-2) C^(-2)))]
  exp(-(log(r/eta)-A)C) <= 1/2.
```

Such a `C_0` exists for every displayed quadruple `(r,eta,A,K)`.  For
`C>=2 eta^(-1)` the denominator is at least `3/4`, so

```text
log R_0(C) = A C - K C log C,
log R_1(C) <= log((20/3)(r/2)^3/r^2)
              + (A-log(r/2))C - K C log C,
log R_2(C) <= log((16/3)(r/eta)^3/r^2) - 2 log C
              - (log(r/eta)-A)C.
```

The first two right-hand sides tend to `-infinity` because `K>0`; the last
tends to `-infinity` because `log(r/eta)-A>0`.  Enlarging `C_0` makes all
three upper bounds at most `log(1/2)`.

For the high-rank part of the remaining classical tail, set

```text
a_G = eta C_G,       c_G = r C_G.
```

For every rank with `C_G>=C_0`, the stable-edge tail in Corollary 24.9 follows
from the two defining-trace estimates

```text
Pr(|T_1(U)| >= sqrt((2r+3eta)C_G)) >= exp(-A C_G),
Pr(tau_G(U) >= a_G) <= exp(-K C_G log C_G).
```

The ranks with `C_G<C_0` are a finite exact-bridge residue after one such
high-rank trace-deviation target is supplied.

Proof.  With the displayed choices, `2C_G/c_G=2/r` and `a_G/c_G=eta/r`.
Since `C_G>=C_0>eta^(-1)`, Chebyshev's factor satisfies

```text
1-a_G^(-2) = 1 - eta^(-2) C_G^(-2) > 0.
```

Proposition 24.10 reduces the tail to

```text
r^2 C_G^2 (1-eta^(-2) C_G^(-2)) (p_1-p_2)
  >= 2(C_G^2+1) p_2 (2/r)^(n_st(G)) + 2(eta/r)^(n_st(G)).
```

Using Lemmas 24.1-24.2 and 24.6, one has `d<=3C_G^2` and

```text
B_b,C_b: n_st(G)=2C_G-1,
D_b:     n_st(G)>=C_G-3.
```

The displayed `R_0` bound and the two trace-deviation estimates give

```text
p_1-p_2 >= (1/2) exp(-A C_G).
```

Hence the left side is at least

```text
(1/2) r^2 C_G^2 (1-eta^(-2) C_G^(-2)) exp(-A C_G).
```

The first term on the right is at most

```text
2(C_G^2+1) exp(-K C_G log C_G) (2/r)^(C_G-3).
```

Dividing this by the displayed lower bound for the left side gives exactly
`R_1(C_G)`, hence it contributes at most one half of the left side.

The second term on the right is at most

```text
2(eta/r)^(C_G-3).
```

Dividing this by the displayed lower bound for the left side gives exactly
`R_2(C_G)`, hence it also contributes at most one half of the left side.
The two right-hand terms are therefore bounded by the left side, so the
Corollary 24.9 comparison holds throughout the high-rank range `C_G>=C_0`.

## Corollary 24.11A (quadratic tau-tail trace-deviation target)

Fix constants `r>2`, `0<eta<1`, and `A>0` with

```text
A < log(r/eta).
```

For an integer `C>eta^(-1)`, put

```text
N(C) = floor(C/2),
H(C) = N(C)^(-5/2) exp(-N(C)/2),
S_eta(C) = 2 exp(-(eta C-1)^2/(16(1+H(C)))).
```

Let `C_0^quad` be an integer with `C_0^quad>eta^(-1)` such that, for every
integer `C>=C_0^quad`,

```text
R_0^quad(C) := exp(A C) S_eta(C) <= 1/2,

R_1^quad(C) :=
  [4(1+C^(-2))(r/2)^3/(r^2(1-eta^(-2) C^(-2)))]
  exp(A C) S_eta(C) (2/r)^C <= 1/2,

R_2^quad(C) :=
  [4(r/eta)^3/(r^2 C^2(1-eta^(-2) C^(-2)))]
  exp(-(log(r/eta)-A)C) <= 1/2.
```

Such a `C_0^quad` exists for every displayed triple `(r,eta,A)`.  Indeed,
`H(C)->0`, so

```text
log R_0^quad(C)
  = log 2 + A C - (eta C-1)^2/(16(1+H(C))),

log R_1^quad(C)
  = log[4(1+C^(-2))(r/2)^3/(r^2(1-eta^(-2) C^(-2)))]
    + A C + log 2 - (eta C-1)^2/(16(1+H(C))) - C log(r/2),

log R_2^quad(C)
  = log[4(r/eta)^3/(r^2 C^2(1-eta^(-2) C^(-2)))]
    - (log(r/eta)-A)C.
```

The first two displayed logarithms tend to `-infty` because `H(C)->0` and
the negative quadratic term dominates, and the last tends to `-infty` because
`log(r/eta)-A>0`.

For the high-rank part of the remaining classical tail, set

```text
a_G = eta C_G,       c_G = r C_G.
```

For every rank with `C_G>=C_0^quad`, the stable-edge tail in Corollary 24.9
follows from the two defining-trace estimates

```text
Pr(|T_1(U)| >= sqrt((2r+3eta)C_G)) >= exp(-A C_G),
Pr(tau_G(U) >= a_G) <= S_eta(C_G).
```

Proof.  Proposition 24.10 reduces the target to

```text
r^2 C_G^2 (1-eta^(-2) C_G^(-2)) (p_1-p_2)
  >= 2(C_G^2+1) p_2 (2/r)^(n_st(G)) + 2(eta/r)^(n_st(G)).
```

The displayed `R_0^quad` bound and the two trace-deviation estimates give

```text
p_1-p_2 >= (1/2) exp(-A C_G).
```

Using `d<=3C_G^2` and `n_st(G)>=C_G-3`, the first right-hand term divided by

```text
(1/2)r^2 C_G^2(1-eta^(-2) C_G^(-2)) exp(-A C_G)
```

is at most `R_1^quad(C_G)`.  The second right-hand term divided by the same
quantity is at most `R_2^quad(C_G)`.  The inequalities
`R_1^quad(C_G),R_2^quad(C_G)<=1/2` therefore absorb both right-hand terms.

## Proposition 24.12 (quadratic bound for the tau-tail)

Fix `0<eta<1/5`.  There is an integer `C_tau^quad(eta)` such that, for every
remaining classical family `G=B_b,C_b,D_b` with `C_G>=C_tau^quad(eta)`,

```text
Pr(tau_G(U) >= eta C_G) <= S_eta(C_G).
```

Proof.  Let `n_*` be the number of nontrivial conjugate eigenvalue pairs in
the defining classical matrix.  Thus `n_*=C_G` for `B_b` and `C_b`, while
`n_*=b` and `C_G=b` or `b-2` for `D_b`; in all cases `n_*>=C_G`.

Apply Courteaut-Johansson-Lambert, Section 4.2, with power `m=2`.  Their
Rains-factorized concentration estimate gives, for `N=floor(n_*/2)` and all
sufficiently large `C_G`,

```text
Pr(|(T_2(U)-E[T_2(U)])/sqrt(2)| >= L)
  <= 2 exp(-L^2/(8(1+N^(-5/2) exp(-N/2)))).
```

The same estimate applies to `-T_2(U)` in the symplectic case.  Moreover,
for the three remaining families, Proposition 5 and Schur orthogonality give

```text
E[tau_G(U)] = 1.
```

Indeed, `E[chi_adj]=0` and `E[T_1^2]=1`; using
`chi_adj=(T_1^2-T_2)/2` in types `B,D` and
`chi_adj=(T_1^2+T_2)/2` in type `C` gives `E[T_2]=1` in types `B,D` and
`E[-T_2]=1` in type `C`.

Hence `tau_G(U)>=eta C_G` implies

```text
|(tau_G(U)-1)/sqrt(2)| >= (eta C_G-1)/sqrt(2).
```

After enlarging `C_tau^quad(eta)`, we have `N>=2`, the admissible range for
the Chernoff parameter in the cited concentration estimate contains
`L=(eta C_G-1)/sqrt(2)`.  Since `n_*>=C_G`,

```text
N=floor(n_*/2) >= floor(C_G/2)=N(C_G),
```

and the function `x^(-5/2) exp(-x/2)` decreases for `x>0`, so

```text
N^(-5/2) exp(-N/2) <= H(C_G).
```

Substituting this `L` in the preceding concentration estimate gives

```text
Pr(tau_G(U) >= eta C_G)
  <= 2 exp(-(eta C_G-1)^2/(16(1+H(C_G)))).
```

## Corollary 24.13 (concrete high-rank positive-trace target)

There is an integer `C_trace` such that, for every remaining classical family
`G=B_b,C_b,D_b` with `C_G>=C_trace`, the stable-edge tail in Corollary 24.9
follows from the single positive-trace lower estimate

```text
Pr(|T_1(U)| >= sqrt((22991901/5000000) C_G))
  >= exp(-(461/200) C_G).
```
